title: Listening - Part 4: Interpreting for Live Audiences
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with a woman called Grace, who works as a sign-language interpreter. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: My final guest today is Grace Bennett, a sign-language interpreter who works at theatres and public events. Grace, when did you first learn sign language? || Grace: My younger brother is deaf, so I began learning when I was a child in order to communicate with him properly. However, I never thought of interpretation as a profession until we attended a public talk together. [24]The interpreter there enabled my brother to follow every joke and question, and I saw how much access the role could provide[/24]. I later took formal training. || Presenter: Is interpreting a theatre performance different from interpreting a speech? || Grace: Very different. At a meeting, accuracy and speed are central. In a play, you also need to represent mood, rhythm and different characters. [25]Before interpreting a production, I attend rehearsals so I understand the actors’ movements and the emotional tone of each scene[/25]. I am not replacing the actors, but my interpretation must connect with their performance. || Presenter: What is most difficult about live events? || Grace: Speakers sometimes depart from a prepared script, which is manageable if the idea is clear. The hardest moments are when several people speak at once. [26]An interpreter has to decide instantly which contribution the audience most needs to understand, because it is impossible to communicate every overlapping word[/26]. That responsibility requires experience. || Presenter: Have you noticed attitudes changing? || Grace: Yes, venues used to arrange interpretation only after someone specifically requested it. Now many plan it from the beginning. [27]When interpretation is advertised in advance, deaf audience members can choose performances confidently rather than having to ask whether access will be available[/27]. That makes the whole experience more welcoming. || Presenter: Does positioning matter in a theatre? || Grace: Extremely. The interpreter must be clearly visible but should not block sightlines or distract unnecessarily. On one production, I was placed far to one side. [28]Audience feedback showed that looking continually from the actors to an interpreter at the edge of the stage made following the story tiring[/28]. After that, the theatre worked more carefully on positioning. || Presenter: Do you use new technology in your work? || Grace: Recorded performances can include interpretation on screen, and streamed events have opened useful possibilities. Yet technology is not automatically accessible. [29]For an online event, I check that the interpreter’s image remains large and clear rather than becoming a tiny box beside the main video[/29]. Visibility is essential. || Presenter: What do you wish hearing audiences understood? || Grace: Interpretation is not a distraction or an extra favour. [30]Providing access from the start allows deaf people to participate independently, without needing to explain their requirements each time[/30]. That should be normal in cultural and public life.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: Why did Grace decide to train as a professional interpreter?

options.A: She wanted to teach her brother sign language.
options.B: She saw how interpretation enabled participation at an event.
options.C: A public speaker encouraged her to apply for training.

questions.explanation: At a public talk, she observed how the interpreter allowed her brother full access.

questions.question: Why does Grace attend rehearsals before interpreting a play?

options.A: To understand movement and emotion within the performance.
options.B: To help actors learn signs needed in their roles.
options.C: To decide whether the language in the play is suitable.

questions.explanation: Rehearsals help her understand actors' movements and the emotional tone.

questions.question: What does Grace find hardest at live events?

options.A: Keeping up when a speaker uses no prepared text.
options.B: Choosing what to interpret when voices overlap.
options.C: Remembering technical vocabulary during long talks.

questions.explanation: When several people talk together, she must quickly decide what is most essential.

questions.question: Why does Grace value venues advertising interpreted performances early?

options.A: It allows deaf audiences to plan attendance confidently.
options.B: It reduces the cost of providing interpreters.
options.C: It helps performers communicate directly with interpreters.

questions.explanation: Advance information lets deaf audience members choose events without having to request access.

questions.question: What problem arose from an interpreter being placed at the side of a stage?

options.A: Actors were unable to see the interpretation.
options.B: Some audience members could not see the stage.
options.C: Following both the acting and interpretation was tiring.

questions.explanation: Feedback reported that looking back and forth across the stage made the story hard to follow.

questions.question: What does Grace check during an online interpreted event?

options.A: That the interpreter is clearly visible on screen.
options.B: That viewers can choose different camera angles.
options.C: That a recording is made for future use.

questions.explanation: She ensures the interpreter's image does not become too small to see clearly.

questions.question: What is Grace's general view of access provision?

options.A: It should depend on whether audiences request it.
options.B: It should be built into events as a normal practice.
options.C: It is most necessary at cultural rather than public events.

questions.explanation: She says access from the beginning should be normal and permit independent participation.
